titleStmt.title: Letter from William Huggard to Edward Carson, 15 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Huggard

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from William Huggard to Edward Carson (1854-1935). Carson was a prominent Unionist Politician. Originally a practicing solicitor from Dublin, Carson's opposition to the proposed third Home Rule bill made him the figure head of Unionism throughout the island. He was the centerpiece of the speaking tour that culminated in Ulster Day (28 September 1912), when just under half a million signed Ulster's solemn league and covenant pledging to use ‘all means which may be found necessary to defeat the present conspiracy to set up a home rule parliament in Dublin’. Carson inaugurated the Larne gunrunning in 1914 which armed the UVF, many of whom Carson would encourage to join the British Army throughout the First World War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
NORWICH UNION FIRE INSURANCE SOCIETY LTD.
A. D. 1797. TELEPHONE NO 95.
TELEGRAPHIC ADDRESS "AGENTS TRALEE"
Estate Office,
Tralee. LATE
HUSSEY, DENNY & HUGGARD,
AGENTS.
NOW WILLIAM HUGGARD, F.S.I. 15th November 1915. Dear Sir, You will be interested to read the enclosed from the pen
of the R. C. Bishop of Limerick. The Loyalists of the South, think
it a great weakness on the part of the Government, at this time of
stress and need, to allow any men of Military age to leave the
Country. May I express the hope that you may see your way to oppose
the Arming of the Nationalist Volunteers for "HOME defence", a course
which the Newspapers suggest is in contemplation. They are anxious
to be Armed and legalised merely as a preparation for the introduction
of Home Rule. Your high Imperial and unselfish principles are greatly ap-
preciated by the Loyal Minority, and this/is my excuse for taking the
liberty of sending the/enclosed. Yours faithfully, W. Huggard. The Right Hon Sir Edward Carson. K. C. M. P.
The House of Commons.
LONDON.
Irish Times
13 NOV. 1915 EMIGRANTS AND THE WAR.
A REMARKABLE LETTER. The Roman Catholic Bishop of Limerick has
sent the following letter to the Munster
News:— SIR, — The treatment which the poor Irish
emigrant lads have received at Liverpool is
enough to make any Irishman's blood boil
with anger and indignation. What wrong
have they done to deserve insults and outrage
at the hands of a brutal English mob? They
do not want to be forced into the English
Army and sent to fight battles in some part
of the world. Is not that within their right?
They are supposed to be freemen, but they
are made to feel that they are prisoners, who
may be compelled to lay down their lives for a
cause that is not worth "three rows of pins"
to them. It is very probable that these poor
Connaught peasants know little or nothing of
the meaning of the war. Their blood is not
stirred by the memories of Kossovo, and they
have no burning desire to die for Serbia.
They would much prefer to be allowed to till
their own potato gardens in peace in Conne-
mara. Small nationalities, and the wrongs of
Belgium and Rheims Cathedral, and all the
other cosmopolitan considerations that rouse
the enthusiasm of the Irish Party, but do not
get enough of recruits in England, are far too
high-flying for uneducated peasants, and it
seems a cruel wrong to attack them because
they cannot rise to the level of the dis-
interested Imperialism of Mr. T. P. O' Connor
and the rest of the New Brigade. But in all the shame and humiliation of this
disgraceful episode, what angers one most is
that there is no one, not even one of their own
countrymen, to stand up and defend them.
Their crime is that they are not ready to die
for England. Why should they? What have
they or their forebears ever got from England
that they should die for her? Mr. Redmond
will say a Home Rule Act on the Statute
Book. But any intelligent Irishman will say
a simulacrum of Home Rule, with an express
notice that it is never to come into operation.
This war may be just or unjust, but any fair-
minded man will admit that it is England's
war, not Ireland's. When it is over, if Eng-
land wins, she will hold a dominant power in
this world, and her manufactures and her
commerce will increase by leaps and bounds.
Win or lose, Ireland will go on, in her old
round of misgovernment, intensified by a
grinding poverty which will make life intoler-
able. Yet the poor fellows who do not see
the advantage of dying for such a cause are to
be insulted as "shirkers" and "cowards,"
and the men whom they have raised to power
and influence have not one word to say on
their behalf. If there is to be conscription,
let it be enforced all round; but it seems to
be the very intensity of injustice to leave
English shirkers by the million go free, and
coerce the small remnant of the Irish race into
a war which they do not understand, and
which, whether it is right or wrong, has but a
secondary and indirect interest for them. — I am, dear sir, your obedient servant,
EDWARD THOMAS,
Bishop of Limerick. 10th November, 1915.
BALKAN WAR
THEATRE.
BULGAR DEFEAT BY
THE FRENCH.
SERBIANS RETREAT-
ING IN GOOD ORDER.</head Telegrams received in Athens confirm the re-
ported defeat of the Bulgarians by the
French near Kuprili, and the great losses
sustained by the enemy. The news is not
officially confirmed, and the Bulgarians
in their latest despatch make no mention
of this battle. The Serbians report that they are retreating
in good order south and east of Kraljevo
and east of Ivanjica. Fierce combats took
place on the left bank of the Southern
Morava. The Germans report that their pursuit of the
Serbians continues south of the line Kral-
jevo Trstenik. (PRESS ASSOCIATION WAR SPECIAL.)
ATHENS, THURSDAY. A telegram from Salonika to the Patris
says the French have inflicted a sever defeat
on the Bulgarians near Kuprili (Veles). The Bulgarians, who sustained immense
losses, demanded a short armistice for the
burying of the dead. The fall of Kuprili is considered imminent.
The Serbian Legation here believes in the
possibility of this news, but there is no official
confirmation. SERBIAN REPORT.
(PRESS ASSOCIATION WAR SPECIAL.)
PARIS, Friday. A serbian official communique issued on
November 10th says:— We occupied in good order the defensive
positions south and east of Kraljevo and east
of Ivanjica, and also positions on the eastern
front, on the left bank of Southern Morava,
where fierce combats took place. BULGARIAN DESPATCH.
(PRESS ASSOCIATION WAR SPECIAL.)
AMSTERDAM, Friday. The Sofia official communique says: — Our troops on the 10th continued the pursuit
of the defeated Serbian Army on the left
bank of the Morava. Fresh booty is dis-
covered daily in the conquered towns. In the Morava valley, along the railway,
we captured to-day four quick-;firing howitzers,
eight quick-firing field guns, with ammunition
carts; eight machine guns.
IRISH VOLUNTEER FORCE
TO COME UNDER NEW BILL.
The "Times" says — It is understood there is a
likelihood the passing into law of Lord Lincoln-
shire's Volunteer Bill. It gives power to the War
Office to accept the services of Volunteer corps in
England and Wales for home defence. The
Nationalists, however, took strong exception to the
omission of Ireland, and to the possible frustration of
Mr Redmond's dream, to which he gave expression
in a memorable speech, of the Nationalist and Ulster
Volunteers defending the coasts of Ireland side by
side, The Government have now acceded to their
wish that the Bill should apply to the United King-;
dom as a whole, and Lord Newton will move
amendments accordingly.
